story: I heard this on 107.7 The End before MTV played it to death. Fifteen years old
column7: sitting on the edge of my bed with the volume just past where it should have been. Half my high school stopped pretending to like the Cure overnight.
ending a relationship I'd outgrown but didn't know how to leave. Eddie Vedder's voice on this song felt like permission to grieve something that had once been good.

story: Listening to Exile in Guyville on cassette in my bedroom felt like a secret being passed to me. Liz wasn't pretty about anything and that was the relief — somebody was finally telling the truth.
Driving east on I-90 toward Snoqualmie Pass with the windows down. The video was creepy in a way that felt fitting for that summer — the last one my parents stayed married.
Courtney was already a mess and everyone knew it
column7: but this song still cut. Heard it loud at Re-bar one Saturday and ended up crying in the bathroom for reasons I couldn't have named.

story: Screamed every word in my best friend's Honda Civic
column8: the summer after we graduated. The catharsis of being that publicly angry felt new and necessary.

album: So Tonight That I Might See
story: Played this on every rainy bus ride from Capitol Hill to campus my freshman year. Still the most Seattle song I know — slow
column9: slightly sad. Hope Sandoval's voice and the weather were the same texture.

story: Saw them at the Crocodile that spring
column7: twenty-one and exhausted from finals. Corin and Carrie's guitars locking together with no bass — just attack. Changed what I thought a band could be.
summer of '97. My best friend and I drove out from Capitol Hill with sleeping bags in the trunk. The whole festival was women on stage; I'd never felt anything like it.

story: Last song I played before driving south to start my first real job. The Pacific Northwest stayed with me even after I left
column7: but this was the version of me who finally felt ready to leave.
